coredata.dc:title: Advantages of ZnO nanotaper photoanodes in photoelectrochemical cells and graphene quantum dot sensitized solar cell applications

Dhar, Saurab
Chakraborty, Pinak
Debnath, Kamalesh
Mondal, Suvra Prakash

ZnO nanotaper
Photoelectrochemical cell
Nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots
Quantum dot sensitized solar cells

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Growth and attachment of nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs)
Fabrication of quantum dot sensitized solar cells

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Top view scanning electron micrographs of (a) ZnO nanorods and ZnO nanotaper samples (b) ZnO NT1, (c) ZnO NT2 and (d) ZnO NT3.
TEM micrograph of (a) ZnO nanorods, (b) single ZnO nanorod, (c) HRTEM image of ZnO NR and (d) SAED pattern of ZnO NRs.
TEM micrograph of (e) ZnO nanotapres grown in 0.2 M urea concentration (NT2), (f) single ZnO NT2, (g) HRTEM image of NT2 and (h) SAED pattern of NT2.
TEM micrograph of (i) ZnO nanotapers grown in 0.5 M urea concentration (NT3), (j) single ZnO Nanorod, (k) HRTEM image of ZnO NT3 and (l) SAED pattern of ZnO NT3.
XRD patterns of ZnO nanorods and ZnO nanotapers (NT1, NT2 and NT3) grown on FTO coated glass substrates. Inset shows the intensity ratio between (002) and (101) planes (I (002)/I (101)).
(a) Full range XPS spectra of ZnO NR and ZnO NT2, (b) High resolution XPS spectra of Zn 2p electronic peak of ZnO NR and ZnO NT2, HRXPS of O1s electronic peak of (c) ZnO NRs and (d) ZnO NT2.

(a) Schematic representation for the formation of ZnO NRs and nanotapers at different urea concentration, (b) Schematic diagram with various planes of a typical wurtzite ZnO structures. (c) Schematic illustration of varies planes ZnO nanorods and ZnO nanotapers.

(a) Linear sweep voltammogram and (b) Photoconversion efficiency vs applied voltage plots of ZnO NR, ZnO NT1, ZnO NT2 and ZnO NT3 photoanodes under illumination, (c) Ammperometric i-t curves of ZnO NR, ZnO NT1, ZnO NT2 and ZnO NT3 photoanodes at 0 V vs. Ag/AgCl, (d) Nyquist Plots (Re Z vs Im Z) of ZnO NR, ZnO NT1, ZnO NT2 and ZnO NT3 photoanodes at 0 V vs. Ag/AgCl under illumination. Equivalent circuit diagram of photoanodes are shown in inset. (e) Photovoltage response and (f) photovoltage decay plots of ZnO NR, ZnO NT1, ZnO NT2 and ZnO NT3 photoanodes under illumination. All measurements were carried out of under illumination of white light of intensity ~ 170 mW/cm2.

(a) Linear sweep voltammograms of ZnO NT2 and NGQDs/ZnO NT2 phototanodes. Photoconversion efficiency vs. applied bias plots are shown at inset. (b) Transient photoresponse plots of ZnO NT2 and NGQDs/ZnO NT2. (c) Nyquist Plots of ZnO NT2 and NGQDs/ZnO NT2 phototanodes. All measurements were carried out under white light illumination of intensity of 170 mW/cm2. (d) IPCE vs wavelength plots of ZnO NT2 and NGQDs/ZnO NT2 measured under zero bias.
Photocurrent retention plots of ZnO NRs, ZnO NT2 and NGQD/ZnO NT2 under constant white illumination of intensity of 170 mW/cm2 at 0 V vs Ag/AgCl.

(a) Schematic diagram of a typical NGQD sensitized ZnO nanotaper based QDSSC device and (b) Current density vs. voltage characteristics of ZnO NRs, ZnO NTs, NGQDs/ZnO NRs and NGQDs/ZnO NTs photoanodes based solar cells under illumination of light of intensity 100 mWcm−2.
Photocurrents and photoconversion efficiencies of some reported ZnO nanorod based photoanodes.

head.ce:title: Advantages of ZnO nanotaper photoanodes in photoelectrochemical cells and graphene quantum dot sensitized solar cell applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, National Institute of Technology, Agartala 799046, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Electronics and Communication Engineering, National Institute of Technology, Agartala 799046, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Electronics and Communication Engineering

ce:section.ce:para: One-dimensional nanostructures, such as nanorods, nanowires, nanotubes are promising candidates in photoelectrochemical (PEC) solar energy conversion due to its high electron mobility (up to ~1000 cm2 V s−1 for a single ZnO nanorod), lower charge carrier loss, favorable band-edge position that straddle the redox potential of photoelectrolysis in water splitting [1–3]. However, low visible absorption due to its large bandgap is the major bottleneck for the further enhancement of solar energy conversion. Without any doping or surface modification, various ZnO nanostructures such as nanopencil, nanoforest, hierarchical nanowires, 3D branched nanowire and nanoflowers have been studied to increase the photoabsorption [3–5]. Zhang et al. [3] studied branched ZnO nanowires photoanode for PEC water-splitting and obtained 0.24% photoconversion efficiency (i.e. photon to hydrogen conversion). Hou et al. [4] fabricated various ZnO nanostructures such as nanorods and nanorods interconnected by nanosheets. The maximum photoconversion efficiency was obtained ~0.2% at potential of 0.77 V (vs. RHE). However, using pristine ZnO nanostructures the highest photoconversion efficiency was achieved upto 0.54% by Zhang et al. using ZnO nanorod/nanoplatelet core-shell arrays [5].

On the other hand, semiconductor quantum dots with narrow band gap such as PbS, CdS, CdSe and CdTe were studied as photosensitizer with ZnO nanostructure photoanodes to extend the photoabsorption in UV–visible-infrared region [6–10]. To the best of our knowledge, the maximum photoconversion efficiency was achieved 6.14% using CdSe/CdS co-sensitized ZnO/WOx nanostructure [11]. However, these chalcogenide semiconductor based photoanodes are highly toxic and not stable in aqueous solution under light illumination. Instead of using toxic semiconductor QDs, recently, 0D carbon nanomaterials like graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been studied as green sensitizers with ZnO, TiO2, α-Fe2O3, SrTiO3 and BiVO4 nanostructures for photodetector, photoelectrochemical cell, solar cell and photocatalytic applications [12–17]. Kim et al. [18] studied the amine passivated carbon dot sensitized ZnO nanostructured based photoanodes which exhibited 4 times enhancement of photocurrent compared to pristine ZnO. Yoon et al. [19] studied GQD decorated CdSe sensitized TiO2 photoanodes for visible light PEC hydrogen production and observed 20% higher photocurrent density (~14.16 mA/cm2) compared to CdSe/TiO2 photoanodes (~12.03 mA/cm2). In the earlier report, we have also observed superior photocurrents, photoconversion efficiency, incident photon-to-current conversion efficiency (IPCE) using GQD and N doped GQD, S, N co doped GQD sensitized ZnO NR based photoanodes [20–22].

In this article, we have synthesized taper-like ZnO nanorods using facile hydrothermal method. The photoelectrochemical properties of taper-like nanorods has been studied in details. To further improve the PEC properties, N doped GQDs (NGQDs) were attached with taper-like ZnO nanorods. The potential application of NGQDs sensitized photoanodes in quantum dot sensitized solar cells (QDSSCs) has been demonstrated.

ZnO nanotapers (ZnO NTs) were gown by hydrothermal method on fluorine doped tin oxide (FTO) coated glass substrates similar to ZnO nanorods growth [23]. During ZnO nanorod synthesis, ZnO nanoparticle seeded layer were dipped in equimolar (0.05 M) zinc nitrate and hexamethylenetetramine(HMTA) solutions at 95 °C for 4 h. In case of ZnO nanotaper growth, 0.1 M, 0.2 M and 0.5 M urea were added to the previous zinc nitrate and HMTA solutions. Afterwards, the as grown nanotaper samples were annealed at 400 °C for 2 h for better crystallinity. In this present study, ZnO NRs grown at 0.1 M, 0.2 M, 0.5 M urea concentration are denoted as NT1, NT2 and NT3, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Growth and attachment of nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: NGQDs were synthesized using hydrothermal process. The detail experimental protocols were reported elsewhere [22]. At first, 1 mmol of citric acid (C6H8O7) (Merck, Germany) and 3 mmol of urea (CH4N2O) (Loba, India) were dissolved in 30 ml de-ionized (DI) water and stirred vigorously for 30 mins. The clear solution was poured in a Teflon-lined stainless still autoclave (50 ml) and the solution was heated up to 180 °C for 8 h. Afterwards, the as grown NGQDs solution was gradually cooled down to room temperature. Finally, NGQDs were collected by adding 150 ml ethanol in the solution and centrifuged at 9000 rpm for 30 mins. ZnO nanotaper samples were dipped in NGQDs solutions for 2 h for attachment. The samples were washed with DI water and heated at 120 °C for 3 h in air for further use.

The scanning electron microscopy (SEM) measurements and energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) analysis of ZnO NTs and NGQDs decorated ZnO NTs were carried out using a ZEISS SUPRA 40 system. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) study was performed using a JEOL JEM-2100F microscope. UV–visible absorption spectra of the samples were measured with a Perkin-Elmer Lambda 950 spectrometer. X-ray diffraction (XRD) study of the nanotaper samples were carried out using a Bruker D8 ADVANCE diffractometer with CuKα (λ = 0.15418 nm) source. The elemental analysis of the samples was investigated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy measurements (XPS) (PHI 5000 Versa Probe II).

Electrochemical measurements were performed by an electrochemical workstation (CHI 660D, USA) in three electrode setup. In electrochemical study, ZnO NTs grown on FTO coated glass substrates were utilized as the working electrodes (photoanodes). A single platinum wire and calibrated Ag/AgCl standard electrode were used as counter and reference electrodes, respectively. In all PEC measurements, 0.1 M aqueous solution of Na2SO4 was used as the electrolyte. All photoanodes were illuminated by monochromatic light (340–500 nm) and broadband light (intensity ~ 170 mW/cm2) from a monochromator and Xenon light source (Sciencetech, Canada).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of quantum dot sensitized solar cells
ce:section.ce:para: NGQDs decorated ZnO NTs were used as photoanodes for solar cell experiments. The counter electrode was prepared by depositing activated platinum solution (Platisol T, Solaronix) on FTO coated glass substrate followed by heating at 450 °C for 30 min. All photoanodes were sealed against electrodes using hot melt plastic spacers (25-μm thick, Solaronix). The active device area of all samples was 0.12 cm2. An iodine based electrolyte solution (Iodolyte Z-50, Solaronix) was served as electrolyte. The current-voltage characteristics of the solar cells were measured using a source meter (B2912A, Agilent, USA) under illumination of 100 mW/cm2 light (AM 1.5) from a solar simulator (Enlitech, Tiwan).

Fig. 1(a) shows the top view SEM image of ZnO NRs grown without urea addition. The micrograph clearly revealed the formation of ZnO NRs perpendicular to substrate. More importantly, nonorods showed a perfect hexagonal structure throughout length. The length and diameter have been estimated from the micrograph and found to be ~1.2 μm and ~140, respectively. Fig. 1(b), 1(c) and 1(d) represent the top view SEM micrographs of ZnO nanotaper grown at 0.1 M, 0.2 M and 0.5 M urea concentrations, respectively. Interestingly, after urea addition, the uniform hexagonal shape throughout the nanorod length, has been modified and taper like ZnO nanorods was formed. Each nanorod consists of a hexagonal shaped rod like structure along with tapering end. The tapering morphology has become dominant with increasing urea concentration. Fig. 2(a) shows plane view TEM images of ZnO NR after detachment from FTO substrates. The average diameter and length of nanorods are 140 nm and 1.2 μm, respectively (Fig. 2(b)). The high resolution TEM (HRTEM) image and selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern of ZnO NRs are presented in Fig. 2(c) and 2(d). ZnO NRs are single crystalline wurtzite structure and the lattice spacing 0.26 nm indicates the growth along (002) direction [22]. Fig. 2(e) represents the TEM micrograph of ZnO nanotaper grown after adding 0.2 M of urea (NT2). The formation of taper-like nanorods has been observed with average diameter ~ 140 nm at the bottom and ~70 nm at taper end (Fig. 2(f)). However, the average length of such taper-like NRs is ~1.8 μm, which is much higher than ZnO NRs. These nanorods were grown along (101) direction with lattice spacing 0.24 nm [24] (Fig. 2(g), 2(h)). Fig. 2(i) shows the TEM image of ZnO NRs (NT3) after adding urea of 0.5 M concentration. The diameter at the bottom and taper end are 110 nm and 45 nm, respectively. The average length of such nanotaper sample (NT3) is ~2.4 μm, which is 2 times higher than ZnO NRs. HRTEM image and SAED pattern of NT3 sample indicated that at higher urea concentration, the nanotaper are less crystalline compared to other sample (Fig. 2(k) and (l)).

Fig. 3 shows the XRD patterns of ZnO nanorods and nanotaper arrays grown on FTO coated glass substrates. All nanorods are highly crystalline, the dominant peaks at (002), (101), (002) planes confirmed the growth of hexagonal wurtzite ZnO (JCPDS card No. 36–1451, a = b = 3.2498 Å, c = 5.2066 Å) [23]. In all samples, the strong XRD peak along (002) direction indicated the growth of preferred c-axis oriented ZnO NRs, which also corroborate our TEM results. Interestingly, after addition of urea in the reaction, the intensity of (002) peak decreases whereas (101) increases. As for example, the intensity ratio of (002) and (101) planes [I (002)/I (101)] for ZnO NRs, NT1, NT2 and NT3 samples are 7.5, 4.5, 3.8 and 2.9, respectively.

Fig. 4(a) describes the full scan XPS spectrum of ZnO NRs and ZnO NTs grown at 0.2 urea concentration (NT2). The XPS spectra show the presence of zinc and oxygen peaks in both nanorods. The high resolution XPS peak of Zn 2p electrons is presented in Fig. 4(b). The Zn 2p binding energy peak has been split into Zn 2p3/2 and Zn 2p1/2 peaks due to spin orbit coupling located at 1021.5 eV and 1044.6 eV, respectively [25]. The spin orbit spilling with energy 23.1 eV, confirmed the presence of Zn atoms in +2 oxidation state in wurtzite ZnO. Fig. 4(c) and 4(d) represent the high resolution XPS spectra of O1s electrons of ZnO NRs and ZnO NTs (NT2), respectively. The broad peak O1s has been deconvoluted into two Gaussians peaks at 530.1 eV and 531.6 eV. The peak at ~530.1 eV is attributed to O2– ions in wurtzite ZnO and the peak at 531.6 eV, represents the presence of oxygen ions (O2−) in oxygen deficiency regions of ZnO, which is nothing but the existence of oxygen vacancies [25]. The oxygen vacancies in ZnO NR and ZnO NT (NT2) were calculated from XPS results and found to be 35.2% and 40.7%, respectively. Our XPS study demonstrated the presence of more oxygen vacancies in ZnO NT samples.

Fig. 5(a) represents the schematic growth process of ZnO NRs and ZnO nanotapers (NT1, NT2, NT3). The formation of taper-like morphology of ZnO nanorods is responsible for unique crystal structure of ZnO [26,27]. In ZnO crystal, tetrahedrally coordinated Zn2+ and O2– ions are stacked alternatively along the c-axis direction (Fig. 5(b)). Typically, the top of ZnO nanorods surfaces are Zn2+ terminated (0001), which are photocatalytically active, on the other hand, bottom surfaces are O2– terminated ( 000
1 ¯
) and chemically inert. Polar surfaces of ZnO are thermodynamically less stable compared to non-polar faces and undergo crystal rearrangement to minimize their surface energy, which results rapid crystal growth. Under hydrothermal process, the growth velocities of ZnO crystal along various directions can be written as
0001
> [ 01
1 ¯
1 ¯
>
01
1 ¯
0
>
01
1 ¯
0
>
000
1 ¯
[24,25]. Fig. 5(c) shows the schematic diagram of a typical hexagonal shaped ZnO nanorods and nanotaper with various crystal planes. It has been observed that, ZnO nanorod growth rate is strongly dependent on the release of OH– ions in the hydrothermal reaction. Addition of urea in the reaction provides extra OH– ions, which accelerate the ZnO crystal growth. At higher growth rate, high energy nonpolar surfaces were disappeared and the crystalline planes with polar surface having lower surface energy
10
1 ¯
1
start to grow more quickly due to the electrostatic interaction between the ions and surface, which results the appearance of taper-like structure [26–29].

To study the role of ZnO NT samples in PEC solar energy conversion, electrochemical measurements were carried out with NT1, NT2 and NT3 samples under dark and illumination of white light of intensity 170 mW/cm2. Fig. 6(a) shows linear sweep voltammogram of all photoanodes. Upon illumination of light, the current density (J) of a typical ZnO NRs based photoanode is 1.23 mAcm−2 at 1 V vs. Ag/AgCl. Interestingly, ZnO nanotapers photoanodes showed significant enhancement of photocurrent at indentical conditions. For example, the photocurrent density for ZnO NT1, ZnO NT2, and ZnO NT3 at 1 V vs. Ag/AgCl bias are found to be 2.05, 2.40 and 1.68 mAcm−2, respectively. Fig. 6(b) describes the photoconversion efficiency (ie photo to hydrogen) plots with respect to applied bias for all photoanodes. The photoconversion efficiencies (η) were calculated using the following relation [22].
(1)
η =
J
1.23 −
V applied
P in
where J is current density, Vapplied is the applied potential vs Ag/AgCl, and Pin is the power of the incident light. The optimum values of η for ZnO NR, ZnO NT1, ZnO NT2 and ZnO NT3 are obtained as 0.32%, 0.51%, 0.74% and 0.45%, respectively. Interestingly, ZnO NT2 sample demonstrated higher efficiency compared to ZnO NR and others. To the best of our knowledge, our ZnO nanotaper (NT2) showed maximum photoconversion efficiency (~0.74%) compared to others reported results as shown in Table 1 . Fig. 6(c) shows the photocurrent transient curve (J-t) of ZnO NRs and nanotapers at 0 V vs. Ag/AgCl under white light illumination. The maximum short circuit current (J
SC ) was obtained ~0.62 mA/cm2 for ZnO NT2 photoanode, which is 1.8 times higher than ZnO NRs.

The interface properties of ZnO photoanodes/electrolyte interface has been studied using electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (Fig.6d). The equivalent circuit diagram at semiconductor/electrolyte interface is shown at inset of Fig. 6(d). The parameters RS, RSC, CSC, R
ct and CH are series resistance, semiconductor resistance, semiconductor depletion layer capacitance, charge transfer resistance and Helmholtz double layer capacitance, respectively. The charge transfer resistances (R
ct ) at semiconductor/electrolyte junctions have been estimated from Nyquist Plots (Re Z vs Im Z) [20,22]. The charge transfer resistance (R
ct ) for pristine nanorods and NT1, NT2 and NT3 samples are 297, 201, 145 and 174 Ω/cm2 respectively. The lower R
ct value in ZnO NT2 is attributed to better charge transfer and lower recombination at interfaces. The parameters RS, Rsc, CSC, R
ct and CH are series resistance, semiconductor resistance, semiconductor depletion layer capacitance, charge transfer resistance and Helmholtz double layer capacitance, respectively.

To investigate the effective charge transport and recombination lifetime of photogenerated carriers at ZnO/electrolyte junctions, open circuit photovoltage decay (OCPVD) measurements were carried out. Under constant illumination at open circuit condition, the photovoltage sharply increases and get saturated due to the equilibrium between electron accumulation and recombination at the junction. When the light illumination is switched off, the open circuit voltage (VOC) undergoes decay due to the recombination of photogenerated electrons with holes. Fig. 6(e) shows the open circuit photovoltage decay curves of NRs and NTs. Interestingly, ZnO nanotaper photoanodes showed higher photovoltage compared to ZnO nanorods. Enhancement of photovoltage implied the increasing of carrier density, fast charge separation and transport in nanotapers at ZnO/electrolyte interface. To elucidate the charge carrier recombination phenomena, recombination lifetime has been calculated using the following eq. [22]
(2)
τ n
= −
k B
T
e
d
V OC
dt
− 1
where τn is the recombination lifetime, kB is the Boltzmann constant, T is the absolute temperature and VOC is the open circuit voltage. Fig. 6(f) shows the variation of recombination lifetime (τn) with VOC for all photoanodes. It is obvious that, the recombination lifetime (τn) is higher for ZnO NT2 photoanodes compared to other photoanodes. In our PEC study, NT2 photoanode demonstrated maximum photoconversion efficiency (η~0.74 %), maximum short circuit current (J
SC ~ 0.62 mA/cm2), minimal charge transfer resistance (Rct ~ 145 Ω/cm2 ) and higher recombination lifetime compared to all others. The decrease of PEC activities for NT3 sample can be explained as follows. Upon illumination, the photogenerated electrons are transported through single crystalline ZnO NRs along the axial direction and collected at the FTO electrode. The enhancement of PEC in taper-like nanorods is due to the presence of electrochemically active planes and longer length which creates more surface area [35]. It has been found that, the PEC activities have been boosted in nanotaper samples when urea concentration varies from 0.1 M to 0.2 M. As urea content increases in the reaction, the growth rate of nanorods is very fast (Fig. 2). The decrease of PEC activities in NT3 samples is attributed to longer length, which results more carrier recombination compared to other photoanodes. The crystallinity is another prerequisite condition for PEC activities of photoanodes. Although, all photoanode were annealed at same temperature (~400 °C), NT3 samples showed poor crystalline behavior compared to others (Fig. 2 (i)).

From the earlier discussions, it is evident that, PEC properties have been increased several order by changing the shape as well as the length of ZnO NRs. To further improve the PEC performance, we have sensitized ZnO NT2 with NGQDs. The attachment of NGQDs on ZnO NT2 was confirmed using EDX analysis (Supporting Information, Fig. S1). Fig. 7(a), represents linear sweep voltammograms of ZnO nanotaper (NT2) and NGQDs decorated nanotaper samples. The plot of photoconversion efficiency with applied bias is presented at the inset. After NGQDs sensitization, the photoconversion efficiency increases upto 1.15%, which is 3.5 times higher than ZnO nanorods. Fig. 7(b) shows amperometric J-t curve of ZnO nanotaper (NT2) before and after attachment with NGQDs. The short circuit current (J
SC ) increases upto 1.04 mA, which is 1.67 times higher than NT2 and 3 times higher than pristine nanorods. The electrochemical impedance results are plotted in Fig. 7(c). The charge transfer resistance has been extracted from EIS results and found to be 112 Ω/cm2 for NGQDs decorated nanorods, which is much lower than other samples.

From the earlier discussions, it is evident that, PEC properties have been increased several order by changing the shape as well as the length of ZnO NRs. To further improve the PEC performance, we have sensitized ZnO NT2 with NGQDs. The attachment of NGQDs on ZnO NT2 was confirmed using EDX analysis (Supporting Information, Fig. S1). Fig. 7(a), represents linear sweep voltammograms of ZnO nanotaper (NT2) and NGQDs decorated nanotaper samples. The plot of photoconversion efficiency with applied bias is presented at the inset. After NGQDs sensitization, the photoconversion efficiency increases upto 1.15%, which is 3.5 times higher than ZnO nanorods. Fig. 7(b) shows amperometric J-t curve of ZnO nanotaper (NT2) before and after attachment with NGQDs. The short circuit current (J
SC ) increases upto 1.04 mA, which is 1.67 times higher than NT2 and 3 times higher than pristine nanorods. The electrochemical impedance results are plotted in Fig. 7(c). The charge transfer resistance has been extracted from EIS results and found to be 112 Ω/cm2 for NGQDs decorated nanorods, which is much lower than other samples.

To study spectral response of photocurrent before and after sensitization of NGQDs, incident photon-to-current conversion efficiency (IPCE) measurements were carried out in the wavelength rage (300–500 nm) without applying any external bias. The mathematical expression of IPCE (%) in terms of incident power (P
λ
), wavelength (λ) and produced photocurrent density (J λ) can be expressed as [22].
(3)
IPCE
%
=
1240
J λ
λ
P λ
× 100

The maximum IPCE was obtained ~74.5% at λ ~ 350 nm, in NGQDs decorated nanotaper, which is 1.7 times higher than nanotaper (NT2) and 2.6 times higher than ZnO NRs (Fig.7(d)). The electrochemical stability of nanotapers before and after sensitization of NGQDs has been studied upon illumination of white light of intensity 170 mW/cm2 (Fig. 8 ). It has been observed that, for ZnO NT2 photoanode, the photocurrent has been decreased by 18.7% from initial value after 10 mins. Interestingly, in case of NGQDs decorated nanotaper sample, the photocurrent has been reduced by only 5.3% at identical condition. In ZnO NT photoanodes, the photoexcited holes are accumulated on the taper surface due to relatively slower interfacial charge transfer properties and create hole-induced anodic corrosion. However, the improvement of PEC stability of NGQDs sensitized ZnO NT is attributed to faster interfacial charge transfer process at ZnO/electrolyte interface due to the presence of NGQDs which act as good holes extractor and lower photocorrosion was observed [22,36]. In our present investigation, the sensitization of NGQDs with nanotaper photoanode has dual advantages. Firstly, due to the superior photoabsoprtion, favorable band alignment and low interfacial resistance at semiconductor/electrolyte junction of NGQDs decorated nanotapers, the PEC properties has been boosted several times. Secondly due to the less electrochemical activities of NGQDs in electrolyte solution, the stability also increases. In comparison with others reported results, our ZnO nanotaper demonstrated superior photocurrent, photoconversion efficiency and IPCE value.

Fabrication of a low cost, eco-friendly, energy efficient and electrochemically stable photoanodes is attractive for dye sensitized as well as quantum dot sensitized solar cell application [37]. In this present study, we have also utilized such photoanodes in quantum dot sensitized solar cell application. Fig. 9 (a) represents schematic diagram of the solar cell and Fig. 9(b) shows the J-V characteristics of the pristine ZnO NRs, NGQDs/ZnO NRs and NGQDs/ZnO NT photoanodes under illumination of light of intensity ~ 100 mWcm−2. The pristine ZnO NR showed short circuit current (J SC) ~0.38 mAcm−2, open circuit voltage (VOC) ~0.31 V and power conversion efficiency (η
p ) ~0.039%. However, NGQDs/ZnO NR and NGQDs/ZnO NT based quantum dot sensitized solar cell device (QDSSC) exhibits J SC ~0.92 mAcm−2, VOC ~ 0.34 V, η
p ~ 0.121%, and J SC ~ 1.92 mAcm−2, VOC ~ 0.35 V, η
p ~ 0.27% respectively. Our QSSC device shows better photoconversion efficiency compared to other reported results [38–43].

In summary, we have grown taper-like ZnO Nanorod photoanodes on FTO coated glass substrates by simply adding urea (0.1 M, 0.2 M and 0.5 M urea) in the chemical reaction. The microstructural study of the ZnO nanotaper samples were examined using SEM and TEM measurements. ZnO nanotapers grown at 0.2 M urea (ZnO NT2) demonstrated superior PEC activities compared to other photoanodes. To further improve the PEC properties NGQDs were attached with ZnO nanotapres (ZnO NT2). Our NGQDs decorated nanotaper demonstrated photoconversion efficiency η ~ 1.15%, J
SC ~ 1.04 mA/cm2, Rct ~ 112 Ω/cm2 and IPCE ≈ 74.5%, which confirmed the best PEC performance among others undoped ZnO nanostructured based photoanodes. Such GQD sensitized photoanodes are highly stable compared to ZnO NRs. We have also demonstrated the photovoltaic performances of such NGQDs sensitized photoanodes in quantum dot sensitized solar cell applications. The typical QDSSC device showed J SC ~ 1.92 mAcm−2, VOC ~ 0.35 V and power conversion efficiency η
p ~ 0.27% which is also better compared to other reported results. Our present study established the potential use of NGQDs decorated ZnO nanotaper based photoanodes for green, low cost quantum dot sensitized solar cell application.

The following is the supplementary data related to this article.
Fig. S1
(a) SEM image of NGQD sensitized ZnO NTs, (b) Chemical mapping of the key elements in NGQDs/ZnO NTs for C-K, N-K, Zn-L and O-K spectrum.
Fig. S1
Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jelechem.2018.02.015.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This present research work was funded by CSIR Extramural Research Grant, Sanction No. 03(1316)/14/EMR-II dated 16/04/2014, Government of India. We acknowledged the central research facility (CRF) of NIT Agartala for UV-VIS-NIR spectroscopy, XRD, AFM SEM characterizations.
